summary: Spec2Vec is a novel spectral similarity score inspired by Word2Vec, a natural language processing algorithm.
Spec2Vec learns fragmental relationships within a large set of spectral data to derive abstract spectral embeddings.
Peaks in mass spectra have no particular order comparable to word order in a document, so Spec2Vec sets window-size to 500 meaning the entire spectrum counts as context.
Continuous bag of words (CBOW) was generally observed to perform better than skip gram for Spec2Vec.
Training a Spec2Vec model with negative sampling (negative = 5) and 15 iterations on AllPositive or up to 50 epochs on UniqueInchikey achieves optimal model performance.
Spec2Vec represents a spectrum as a weighted sum of its fragment and loss word vectors.
Neutral losses between 5.0 and 200.0 Da were added as features in Spec2Vec document representation.
Peaks are represented in Spec2Vec documents with their position up to a defined decimal precision, using a binning of two decimals.
Spec2Vec takes relationships between fragments into account rather than relying only on binary assessment of each fragment match.
Spec2Vec can estimate the impact of unknown words (peaks) by assessing the uncovered weighted part of a spectrum.
By setting a missing fraction threshold at less than 0.05, Spec2Vec similarity scores for spectra far outside the learned peaks can be avoided.
